content.text: 我遇到一个问题,当subagent内部的agent loop发生异常中断(例如llm api请求返回了400错误),前端无法感知.请检查deepagent的subagentMiddleWare实现,解决这个问题
Let me examine the relevant code to understand the problem.

input.description: Explore subagent middleware and error handling
input.prompt:
Find and read all relevant code related to SubAgentMiddleware in the deepagents SDK and how subagent errors are handled. Look in node_modules/deepagents for the SubAgentMiddleware implementation. Also look at:
1. multi-agent/src/agents/simple-deep-agent/index.ts - the simple deep agent implementation
2. multi-agent/src/agents/agents.service.ts - how events are streamed
3. Any error handling in the subagent/middleware code

Be very thorough - read the full SubAgentMiddleware source code, especially how it handles errors during agent.invoke() calls within the subagent execution loop.

543→//#region src/middleware/subagents.d.ts
   544→/**
   545→ * Default system prompt for subagents.
   546→ * Provides a minimal base prompt that can be extended by specific subagent configurations.
   547→ */
   548→declare const DEFAULT_SUBAGENT_PROMPT = "In order to complete the objective that the user asks of you, you have access to a number of standard tools.";
   549→/**
   550→ * Default description for the general-purpose subagent.
   551→ * This description is shown to the model when selecting which subagent to use.
   552→ */
   553→declare const DEFAULT_GENERAL_PURPOSE_DESCRIPTION = "General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for files and content, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. This agent has access to all tools as the main agent.";
   554→/**
   555→ * System prompt section that explains how to use the task tool for spawning subagents.
   556→ *
   557→ * This prompt is automatically appended to the main agent's system prompt when
   558→ * using `createSubAgentMiddleware`. It provides guidance on:
   559→ * - When to use the task tool
   560→ * - Subagent lifecycle (spawn → run → return → reconcile)
   561→ * - When NOT to use the task tool
   562→ * - Best practices for parallel task execution
   563→ *
   564→ * You can provide a custom `systemPrompt` to `createSubAgentMiddleware` to override
   565→ * or extend this default.
   566→ */
   567→declare const TASK_SYSTEM_PROMPT = "## `task` (subagent spawner)\n\nYou have access to a `task` tool to launch short-lived subagents that handle isolated tasks. These agents are ephemeral \u2014 they live only for the duration of the task and return a single result.\n\nWhen to use the task tool:\n- When a task is complex and multi-step, and can be fully delegated in isolation\n- When a task is independent of other tasks and can run in parallel\n- When a task requires focused reasoning or heavy token/context usage that would bloat the orchestrator thread\n- When sandboxing improves reliability (e.g. code execution, structured searches, data formatting)\n- When you only care about the output of the subagent, and not the intermediate steps (ex. performing a lot of research and then returned a synthesized report, performing a series of computations or lookups to achieve a concise, relevant answer.)\n\nSubagent lifecycle:\n1. **Spawn** \u2192 Provide clear role, instructions, and expected output\n2. **Run** \u2192 The subagent completes the task autonomously\n3. **Return** \u2192 The subagent provides a single structured result\n4. **Reconcile** \u2192 Incorporate or synthesize the result into the main thread\n\nWhen NOT to use the task tool:\n- If you need to see the intermediate reasoning or steps after the subagent has completed (the task tool hides them)\n- If the task is trivial (a few tool calls or simple lookup)\n- If delegating does not reduce token usage, complexity, or context switching\n- If splitting would add latency without benefit\n\n## Important Task Tool Usage Notes to Remember\n- Whenever possible, parallelize the work that you do. This is true for both tool_calls, and for tasks. Whenever you have independent steps to complete - make tool_calls, or kick off tasks (subagents) in parallel to accomplish them faster. This saves time for the user, which is incredibly important.\n- Remember to use the `task` tool to silo independent tasks within a multi-part objective.\n- You should use the `task` tool whenever you have a complex task that will take multiple steps, and is independent from other tasks that the agent needs to complete. These agents are highly competent and efficient.";
   568→/**
   569→ * Type definitions for pre-compiled agents.
   570→ *
   571→ * @typeParam TRunnable - The type of the runnable (ReactAgent or Runnable).
   572→ *   When using `createAgent` or `createDeepAgent`, this preserves the middleware
   573→ *   types for type inference. Uses `ReactAgent<any>` to accept agents with any
   574→ *   type configuration (including DeepAgent instances).
   575→ */
   576→interface CompiledSubAgent<TRunnable extends ReactAgent<any> | Runnable = ReactAgent<any> | Runnable> {
   577→  /** The name of the agent */
   578→  name: string;
   579→  /** The description of the agent */
   580→  description: string;
   581→  /** The agent instance */
   582→  runnable: TRunnable;
   583→}
   584→/**
   585→ * Specification for a subagent that can be dynamically created.
   586→ *
   587→ * When using `createDeepAgent`, subagents automatically receive a default middleware
   588→ * stack (todoListMiddleware, filesystemMiddleware, summarizationMiddleware, etc.) before
   589→ * any custom `middleware` specified in this spec.
   590→ *
   591→ * Required fields:
   592→ * - `name`: Identifier used to select this subagent in the task tool
   593→ * - `description`: Shown to the model for subagent selection
   594→ * - `systemPrompt`: The system prompt for the subagent
   595→ *
   596→ * Optional fields:
   597→ * - `model`: Override the default model for this subagent
   598→ * - `tools`: Override the default tools for this subagent
   599→ * - `middleware`: Additional middleware appended after defaults
   600→ * - `interruptOn`: Human-in-the-loop configuration for specific tools
   601→ * - `skills`: Skill source paths for SkillsMiddleware (e.g., `["/skills/user/", "/skills/project/"]`)
   602→ *
   603→ * @example
   604→ * ```typescript
   605→ * const researcher: SubAgent = {
   606→ *   name: "researcher",
   607→ *   description: "Research assistant for complex topics",
   608→ *   systemPrompt: "You are a research assistant.",
   609→ *   tools: [webSearchTool],
   610→ *   skills: ["/skills/research/"],
   611→ * };
   612→ * ```
   613→ */
   614→interface SubAgent {
   615→  /** Identifier used to select this subagent in the task tool */
   616→  name: string;
   617→  /** Description shown to the model for subagent selection */
   618→  description: string;
   619→  /** The system prompt to use for the agent */
   620→  systemPrompt: string;
   621→  /** The tools to use for the agent (tool instances, not names). Defaults to defaultTools */
   622→  tools?: StructuredTool[];
   623→  /** The model for the agent. Defaults to defaultModel */
   624→  model?: LanguageModelLike | string;
   625→  /** Additional middleware to append after default_middleware */
   626→  middleware?: readonly AgentMiddleware$1[];
   627→  /** Human-in-the-loop configuration for specific tools. Requires a checkpointer. */
   628→  interruptOn?: Record<string, boolean | InterruptOnConfig>;
   629→  /**
   630→   * Skill source paths for SkillsMiddleware.
   631→   *
   632→   * List of paths to skill directories (e.g., `["/skills/user/", "/skills/project/"]`).
   633→   * When specified, the subagent will have its own SkillsMiddleware that loads skills
   634→   * from these paths. This allows subagents to have different skill sets than the main agent.
   635→   *
   636→   * Note: Custom subagents do NOT inherit skills from the main agent by default.
   637→   * Only the general-purpose subagent inherits the main agent's skills.
   638→   *
   639→   * @example
   640→   * ```typescript
   641→   * const researcher: SubAgent = {
   642→   *   name: "researcher",
   643→   *   description: "Research assistant",
   644→   *   systemPrompt: "You are a researcher.",
   645→   *   skills: ["/skills/research/", "/skills/web-search/"],
   646→   * };
   647→   * ```
   648→   */
   649→  skills?: string[];
   650→}
   651→/**
   652→ * Base specification for the general-purpose subagent.
   653→ *
   654→ * This constant provides the default configuration for the general-purpose subagent
   655→ * that is automatically included when `generalPurposeAgent: true` (the default).
   656→ *
   657→ * The general-purpose subagent:
   658→ * - Has access to all tools from the main agent
   659→ * - Inherits skills from the main agent (when skills are configured)
   660→ * - Uses the same model as the main agent (by default)
   661→ * - Is ideal for delegating complex, multi-step tasks
   662→ *
   663→ * You can spread this constant and override specific properties when creating
   664→ * custom subagents that should behave similarly to the general-purpose agent:
   665→ *
   666→ * @example
   667→ * ```typescript
   668→ * import { GENERAL_PURPOSE_SUBAGENT, createDeepAgent } from "@anthropic/deepagents";
   669→ *
   670→ * // Use as-is (automatically included with generalPurposeAgent: true)
   671→ * const agent = createDeepAgent({ model: "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929" });
   672→ *
   673→ * // Or create a custom variant with different tools
   674→ * const customGP: SubAgent = {
   675→ *   ...GENERAL_PURPOSE_SUBAGENT,
   676→ *   name: "research-gp",
   677→ *   tools: [webSearchTool, readFileTool],
   678→ * };
   679→ *
   680→ * const agent = createDeepAgent({
   681→ *   model: "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929",
   682→ *   subagents: [customGP],
   683→ *   // Disable the default general-purpose agent since we're providing our own
   684→ *   // (handled automatically when using createSubAgentMiddleware directly)
   685→ * });
   686→ * ```
   687→ */
   688→declare const GENERAL_PURPOSE_SUBAGENT: Pick<SubAgent, "name" | "description" | "systemPrompt">;
   689→/**
   690→ * Options for creating subagent middleware
   691→ */
   692→interface SubAgentMiddlewareOptions {
   693→  /** The model to use for subagents */
   694→  defaultModel: LanguageModelLike | string;
   695→  /** The tools to use for the default general-purpose subagent */
   696→  defaultTools?: StructuredTool[];
   697→  /** Default middleware to apply to custom subagents (WITHOUT skills from main agent) */
   698→  defaultMiddleware?: AgentMiddleware$1[] | null;
   699→  /**
   700→   * Middleware specifically for the general-purpose subagent (includes skills from main agent).
   701→   * If not provided, falls back to defaultMiddleware.
   702→   */
   703→  generalPurposeMiddleware?: AgentMiddleware$1[] | null;
   704→  /** The tool configs for the default general-purpose subagent */
   705→  defaultInterruptOn?: Record<string, boolean | InterruptOnConfig> | null;
   706→  /** A list of additional subagents to provide to the agent */
   707→  subagents?: (SubAgent | CompiledSubAgent)[];
   708→  /** Full system prompt override */
   709→  systemPrompt?: string | null;
   710→  /** Whether to include the general-purpose agent */
   711→  generalPurposeAgent?: boolean;
   712→  /** Custom description for the task tool */
   713→  taskDescription?: string | null;
   714→}
   715→/**
   716→ * Create subagent middleware with task tool
   717→ */
   718→declare function createSubAgentMiddleware(options: SubAgentMiddlewareOptions): AgentMiddleware$1<undefined, undefined, unknown, readonly [langchain.DynamicStructuredTool<z.ZodObject<{
   719→  description: z.ZodString;
   720→  subagent_type: z.ZodString;
   721→}, z.core.$strip>, {
   722→  description: string;
   723→  subagent_type: string;
   724→}, {
   725→  description: string;
   726→  subagent_type: string;
   727→}, string | Command<unknown, Record<string, unknown>, string>, "task">]>;
   728→//#endregion
   729→//#region src/middleware/patch_tool_calls.d.ts
   730→/**
   731→ * Create middleware that patches dangling tool calls in the messages history.
   732→ *
   733→ * When an AI message contains tool_calls but subsequent messages don't include
   734→ * the corresponding ToolMessage responses, this middleware adds synthetic
   735→ * ToolMessages saying the tool call was cancelled.
   736→ *
   737→ * This middleware patches in two places:
   738→ * 1. `beforeAgent`: Patches state at the start of the agent loop (handles most cases)
   739→ * 2. `wrapModelCall`: Patches the request right before model invocation (handles
   740→ *    edge cases like HITL rejection during graph resume where state updates from
   741→ *    beforeAgent may not be applied in time)
   742→ *
   743→ * @returns AgentMiddleware that patches dangling tool calls
   744→ *
   745→ * @example
   746→ * ```typescript
   747→ * import { createAgent } from "langchain";
   748→ * import { createPatchToolCallsMiddleware } from "./middleware/patch_tool_calls";
   749→ *
   750→ * const agent = createAgent({
   751→ *   model: "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929",
   752→ *   middleware: [createPatchToolCallsMiddleware()],
   753→ * });
   754→ * ```
   755→ */
   756→declare function createPatchToolCallsMiddleware(): AgentMiddleware<undefined, undefined, unknown, readonly (_langchain_core_tools0.ClientTool | _langchain_core_tools0.ServerTool)[]>;
   757→//#endregion
   758→//#region src/backends/state.d.ts
   759→/**
   760→ * Backend that stores files in agent state (ephemeral).
   761→ *
   762→ * Uses LangGraph's state management and checkpointing. Files persist within
   763→ * a conversation thread but not across threads. State is automatically
   764→ * checkpointed after each agent step.
   765→ *
   766→ * Special handling: Since LangGraph state must be updated via Command objects
   767→ * (not direct mutation), operations return filesUpdate in WriteResult/EditResult
   768→ * for the middleware to apply via Command.
   769→ */
   770→declare class StateBackend implements BackendProtocol {
   771→  private stateAndStore;
   772→  constructor(stateAndStore: StateAndStore);
   773→  /**
   774→   * Get files from current state.
   775→   */
   776→  private getFiles;
   777→  /**
   778→   * List files and directories in the specified directory (non-recursive).
   779→   *
   780→   * @param path - Absolute path to directory
   781→   * @returns List of FileInfo objects for files and directories directly in the directory.
   782→   *          Directories have a trailing / in their path and is_dir=true.
   783→   */
   784→  lsInfo(path: string): FileInfo[];
   785→  /**
   786→   * Read file content with line numbers.
   787→   *
   788→   * @param filePath - Absolute file path
   789→   * @param offset - Line offset to start reading from (0-indexed)
   790→   * @param limit - Maximum number of lines to read
   791→   * @returns Formatted file content with line numbers, or error message

1. multi-agent/src/agents/simple-deep-agent/index.ts - the simple deep agent implementation

content.text: Let me look at the specific files more carefully.
Let me look at the stream handlers and how subagent events are tracked.

Now I understand the problem. The `dispatchStreamEvent` handler handles `on_tool_error` but does **NOT** handle `on_chain_error` or `on_chat_model_error`. When the subagent's LLM call fails with a 400 error:

1. `on_chat_model_error` fires → **not handled** → no `assistant-end` event
2. `on_chain_error` fires → **not handled** → no `agent-end` event
3. Frontend never receives end events for the subagent → shows "RUNNING" forever

The fix is to handle these error events in `stream-handlers.ts`.
Now I have the full picture. Let me add handlers for `on_chain_error` and `on_chat_model_error` in the stream handlers.

+  return makeEvent('assistant-end', node, { node, error: message }, subagent);

content.text:
Found it. The `isSubagentCompleted` function (line 61-64) only checks if the last message is a `tool-call-result` for the `task` tool. When the subagent crashes:

1. The backend now emits `agent-end` with error data for the subagent
2. But `isSubagentCompleted` doesn't consider this — it only looks for `tool-call-result` + `task`

The `tool-call-result` for the `task` tool will eventually arrive (because `ToolErrorMiddleware` converts the error to a ToolMessage), but the real issue is that before my fix, the subagent's internal error events were never emitted at all, so the frontend had no indication of what went wrong.

Let me also check if the `tool-call-result` for the `task` tool does arrive with error info when ToolErrorMiddleware handles it.

Looking at the flow again:
1. Subagent LLM call fails with 400 → now we emit `assistant-end` with error + `agent-end` with error
2. `subagent.invoke()` throws → ToolErrorMiddleware catches → returns error ToolMessage  
3. `on_tool_end` fires for `task` tool → `handleToolEnd` processes it → emits `tool-call-result` with error

The `tool-call-result` for `task` should work. But the issue is also that the frontend might need to show the error state. Let me update `isSubagentCompleted` to also detect error end states, and update the preview to show error info.

content.text: Wait, let me reconsider. The `tool-call-result` for the `task` tool should still arrive even on error (thanks to ToolErrorMiddleware). Let me check the `Message` type to see if it has an `error` field.

content.text: Good, there's an `isError` field. Let me check how `messageProcessor` sets this.

Good, the `isError` flag is set when `data.error` is present. So when the `task` tool returns with an error (via ToolErrorMiddleware), the `tool-call-result` event will have `error` data, and `isError` will be set on the message.

Now let me update `subagentGroup.ts` to use this for the display state:
I need to update the `SubagentDisplayState` interface to include an `error` field, and update the display logic:

content.text:
Here's a summary of the changes:

## Problem

When a subagent's internal agent loop encounters an error (e.g., LLM API returns 400), the `streamEvents` API emits `on_chat_model_error` and `on_chain_error` events, but `dispatchStreamEvent` didn't handle them. The frontend never received `assistant-end` or `agent-end` events for the failed subagent, so it stayed in "RUNNING" state forever.

## Changes

### Backend: `stream-handlers.ts`
- Added `handleChatModelError()` — emits `assistant-end` with error info when a model call fails
- Added `handleChainError()` — emits `agent-end` with error info when an agent chain fails  
- Registered both in `dispatchStreamEvent` switch: `on_chain_error` and `on_chat_model_error`

This ensures the frontend receives proper end events with error data even when the subagent crashes.

### Frontend: `subagentGroup.ts`
- Added `error` field to `SubagentDisplayState`
- Added `hasSubagentError()` — checks if any `tool-call-result` for `task` has `isError` set
- Preview shows "执行失败" when error is detected

### Frontend: `MessageList.tsx`
- Added `error` field to `AssistantContentView` subagent type
- Error state renders with `text-destructive` color on preview text
- Card border shows `border-destructive/40 bg-destructive/5` on error
- Stops pulse animation when error occurred
